facts_disclosed: Belgian chocolate maker offered 1,200 cedis/bag for 20 bags direct

turns.content: good evening my friend. something happened this week that I cannot stop thinking about. I need to talk it through
Good evening. I'm here — go ahead and share what's on your mind.
a European man came to the village on Tuesday. from Belgium. he has a chocolate company, small one, he says artisan. he walked the farm with me, tasted the beans, said the quality is excellent
That's interesting — a craft chocolate maker coming all the way to your village to source beans directly. That's a sign your cocoa quality is getting noticed. What did he want exactly?
he wants to buy directly from me. 20 bags. he offered 1,200 cedis per bag my friend. 1,200. that is 240 more than Kuapa pays us. for 20 bags that is 4,800 cedis extra in my pocket

That's a significant premium — 4,800 cedis extra is real money. At 1,200 per bag for 20 bags, that's 24,000 cedis total compared to 19,200 through your current buyer.

But I can hear something in how you're telling me this. You don't sound like a man celebrating a good deal. What's holding you back?

you hear correctly. I am the cooperative chairman. 147 families sell through Kuapa together. we negotiated that contract together. if I, the chairman, sell 20 bags outside the cooperative what message does that send. 'do as I say but not as I do' — my father would be ashamed

That's a real dilemma. You're not just any farmer who got a good offer — you're the one who's supposed to hold the cooperative together. If the chairman sells outside the system, it undermines everything you've built.

But 4,800 cedis is a lot of money for your family. Have you talked to anyone else in the village about this?

only Akosua. she says take the money. she says the children need things. she is not wrong my friend. Kwabena's school fees, Kofi Jr starting school soon, Yaa needs new uniforms. the money would help. but Akosua thinks as a mother. I must think as a chairman also

Akosua's thinking is completely valid — a mother looks at her children's needs and sees the answer clearly. And your thinking is valid too — a chairman looks at 147 families and sees the consequences.

Let me ask this: is there any way to bring this Belgian buyer to the cooperative instead of just to you? If he wants quality cocoa and your village produces it, maybe the whole cooperative could benefit from a direct trade relationship?

I thought about this. but he is small. he says he only needs 20 bags per year. maybe 30 in future. the cooperative produces over 2,000 bags. he cannot buy from everyone. and if I bring him to the cooperative meeting the other farmers will say why does Kofi get the 20 bags and not us

That's a fair point. 20-30 bags is a drop in the ocean for a 2,000-bag cooperative. You can't split that evenly among 147 families — it would be meaningless per family. And selecting which families get the premium price would create resentment no matter how you do it.

So you're really stuck between two things: the money your family genuinely needs, and the principle that holds the cooperative together. Have you told the Belgian buyer you need time to think?

yes. he gave me his phone number and email. he says he will come back in February. he is patient. he says he works with small farmers in other countries the same way. one farmer, direct relationship, no middleman

At least you have time before February. That's good — you don't need to rush this.

A few things to consider as you think:

- **The precedent**: If you sell outside, and other farmers hear the Belgian is paying 1,200, they'll want the same. But he only wants 20 bags. You'd be the one who opened a door that only fits one person.
- **The relationship with Kuapa**: Does your contract prevent members from selling outside? If they find out, could it affect the cooperative's terms?
- **The long view**: Is this a one-time windfall, or could it grow into something that benefits more families eventually?

There's no easy answer here. Both choices have real costs.

the contract does not specifically forbid it. but the understanding is that we sell together. that is the whole point. obi nnim what tomorrow holds. maybe he comes back and offers to buy from the cooperative as a whole. maybe he disappears. maybe the price from Kuapa goes up and the difference is not worth the trouble

You're being honest about the uncertainty, and that's wise. You don't know how any of those paths play out.

What I notice is that you keep coming back to the principle, not the money. The 4,800 cedis is real, but it sounds like the cost to your conscience and your leadership would be just as real. That tells me something about where your heart is — but only you can weigh those scales.

You have until February. You don't need to decide tonight.

that is true. I am still thinking. for now I sell everything through the cooperative as always. nothing has changed yet. but the offer sits in my mind like a stone in a shoe my friend. I cannot ignore it and I cannot be comfortable with it

"A stone in a shoe" — that's exactly what a hard dilemma feels like. You can't walk away from it and you can't walk comfortably with it.

For what it's worth, the fact that you're wrestling with this instead of just taking the money says a lot about who you are as a leader. Take your time. Talk to someone you trust in the village — maybe one of the elders. Sometimes a third voice helps when your head and heart disagree.

I'm here if you want to think through it more before February.
medaase. yes maybe I speak to Elder Osei. he has seen everything in this village. Nyame will guide me. good evening my friend
Elder Osei sounds like the right person. Good evening, and I hope some clarity comes to you in the days ahead. Take care.
